REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Key reproducibility elements are missing: hyperparameters and model configurations are not reported and stochastic training details are only acknowledged as a limitation, so experiments cannot be rerun faithfully. The preprocessing pipeline and evaluation metrics/procedures are not specified, and code is not provided; although the British National Corpus is a public dataset, the paper does not describe how it was extracted or tokenized. Given these gaps, reimplementing the exact experiments would be difficult and results hard to validate or reproduce. | B(2): From the provided summary important experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model configurations are not reported, and preprocessing steps are not clearly described. While the British National Corpus is a publicly known resource, specifics of the subset, tokenization, and tag alignment are unspecified; code is not mentioned and evaluation metrics/procedures are not defined. These omissions make faithful reproduction difficult without contacting the authors.

reviewer_a.justification: Key reproducibility elements are missing: hyperparameters and model configurations are not reported and stochastic training details are only acknowledged as a limitation, so experiments cannot be rerun faithfully. The preprocessing pipeline and evaluation metrics/procedures are not specified, and code is not provided; although the British National Corpus is a public dataset, the paper does not describe how it was extracted or tokenized. Given these gaps, reimplementing the exact experiments would be difficult and results hard to validate or reproduce.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary important experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model configurations are not reported, and preprocessing steps are not clearly described. While the British National Corpus is a publicly known resource, specifics of the subset, tokenization, and tag alignment are unspecified; code is not mentioned and evaluation metrics/procedures are not defined. These omissions make faithful reproduction difficult without contacting the authors.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The topic—using distributional embeddings to probe PoS information—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the venue audience, but the experimental presentation as summarized lacks key standards expected at top venues. There are no baselines, no reported evaluation metrics or statistical analyses, no hyperparameters or code release, and experiments are limited to the BNC/English, which raises reproducibility and robustness concerns. With stronger empirical rigor (clear metrics, baselines/ablations, seeds/hyperparameters, and code) this would be a good fit; as-is it feels borderline. | B(3): The paper’s topic—using distributional semantic models to probe part-of-speech structure—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the venue’s audience of computational linguists and representation learning researchers. However, from the supplied summary the experimental and evaluation setup appears under-specified for a top-tier venue (no baselines, metrics, hyperparameters, or code reported and experiments limited to English/BNC), which weakens methodological rigor expected at those conferences. The problem formulation and positioning (coverage of related work is adequate) are appropriate, but the limited evaluation and single-corpus focus make the submission borderline in fit.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—using distributional embeddings to probe PoS information—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the venue audience, but the experimental presentation as summarized lacks key standards expected at top venues. There are no baselines, no reported evaluation metrics or statistical analyses, no hyperparameters or code release, and experiments are limited to the BNC/English, which raises reproducibility and robustness concerns. With stronger empirical rigor (clear metrics, baselines/ablations, seeds/hyperparameters, and code) this would be a good fit; as-is it feels borderline.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s topic—using distributional semantic models to probe part-of-speech structure—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the venue’s audience of computational linguists and representation learning researchers. However, from the supplied summary the experimental and evaluation setup appears under-specified for a top-tier venue (no baselines, metrics, hyperparameters, or code reported and experiments limited to English/BNC), which weakens methodological rigor expected at those conferences. The problem formulation and positioning (coverage of related work is adequate) are appropriate, but the limited evaluation and single-corpus focus make the submission borderline in fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): As a reproducibility-focused reviewer, I find the paper’s empirical comparison inadequate: no baselines are reported (e.g., standard supervised PoS taggers or prior embedding-based PoS induction methods), and evaluation metrics and conditions are not specified, so fairness cannot be assessed. The authors do note related work is covered, but they do not demonstrate whether prior methods perform comparably or better, nor provide hyperparameters or code to reproduce comparisons. These omissions make it impossible to judge the claimed advantages of using BNC-trained embeddings over established approaches. | B(1): The summary indicates no baselines were mentioned, so the paper fails to compare against appropriate or recent methods for PoS prediction or soft-category modeling. Without baseline experiments, fairness of comparisons and whether prior methods perform comparably cannot be assessed. The write-up appears to discuss related work adequately, but it does not present clear analyses contrasting the proposed classifiers with competing approaches, leaving key comparative questions unanswered.

reviewer_a.justification: As a reproducibility-focused reviewer, I find the paper’s empirical comparison inadequate: no baselines are reported (e.g., standard supervised PoS taggers or prior embedding-based PoS induction methods), and evaluation metrics and conditions are not specified, so fairness cannot be assessed. The authors do note related work is covered, but they do not demonstrate whether prior methods perform comparably or better, nor provide hyperparameters or code to reproduce comparisons. These omissions make it impossible to judge the claimed advantages of using BNC-trained embeddings over established approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates no baselines were mentioned, so the paper fails to compare against appropriate or recent methods for PoS prediction or soft-category modeling. Without baseline experiments, fairness of comparisons and whether prior methods perform comparably cannot be assessed. The write-up appears to discuss related work adequately, but it does not present clear analyses contrasting the proposed classifiers with competing approaches, leaving key comparative questions unanswered.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The experiments demonstrate an interesting proof-of-concept on the BNC but are too narrow to fully support the breadth of the claims: only a single corpus (English BNC) was used, no baselines or evaluation metrics are reported, and key reproducibility details (hyperparameters, seeds, code) are missing. There are no ablation or sensitivity analyses probing corpus size, embedding architecture, tagset choice, or stochastic variability, which are essential given the claims about distributed vs. concentrated PoS signal. Related work is said to be adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but the empirical evaluation lacks the rigor and controls I would expect to trust and reproduce the findings. | B(2): The experiments show promising directions (BNC-trained embeddings and classifiers revealing graded PoS affiliations and annotation inconsistencies), but are shallow for the claims: they use only English (BNC), report no baselines, no evaluation metrics, and do not provide hyperparameters or reproducibility details. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are presented to support the claim that PoS information is distributed across many vector components. Related work is adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but methodological gaps and missing quantitative evaluation materially weaken the evidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments demonstrate an interesting proof-of-concept on the BNC but are too narrow to fully support the breadth of the claims: only a single corpus (English BNC) was used, no baselines or evaluation metrics are reported, and key reproducibility details (hyperparameters, seeds, code) are missing. There are no ablation or sensitivity analyses probing corpus size, embedding architecture, tagset choice, or stochastic variability, which are essential given the claims about distributed vs. concentrated PoS signal. Related work is said to be adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but the empirical evaluation lacks the rigor and controls I would expect to trust and reproduce the findings.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments show promising directions (BNC-trained embeddings and classifiers revealing graded PoS affiliations and annotation inconsistencies), but are shallow for the claims: they use only English (BNC), report no baselines, no evaluation metrics, and do not provide hyperparameters or reproducibility details. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are presented to support the claim that PoS information is distributed across many vector components. Related work is adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but methodological gaps and missing quantitative evaluation materially weaken the evidence.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper offers an interesting empirical exploration of how distributional embeddings encode PoS information and uses classifier errors to surface annotation inconsistencies, but the core idea—using word embeddings for PoS prediction—is well-established. Claims about information being distributed across many vector components and about graded PoS affiliations are useful observations but represent incremental insights rather than a radical new method. Given the absence of baselines, hyperparameter/code reporting, and experiments limited to the BNC, the contribution reads as a moderate, empirical extension rather than a genuinely novel paradigm that would surprise experts. | B(3): The paper applies well-established tools (word embeddings + classifiers) to the long-studied problem of PoS affiliation and uses misclassification analysis to probe annotation inconsistencies; this framing is useful but not fundamentally new. The claim that PoS information is distributed across many vector components and the emphasis on graded/‘soft’ affiliations offer a noteworthy empirical perspective, but it appears as a moderate extension rather than a novel method given prior work using distributional models for PoS induction and soft categories. Limited scope (only the BNC, no baselines or cross-linguistic validation reported) further suggests the contribution is incremental rather than a breakthrough that would surprise experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers an interesting empirical exploration of how distributional embeddings encode PoS information and uses classifier errors to surface annotation inconsistencies, but the core idea—using word embeddings for PoS prediction—is well-established. Claims about information being distributed across many vector components and about graded PoS affiliations are useful observations but represent incremental insights rather than a radical new method. Given the absence of baselines, hyperparameter/code reporting, and experiments limited to the BNC, the contribution reads as a moderate, empirical extension rather than a genuinely novel paradigm that would surprise experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies well-established tools (word embeddings + classifiers) to the long-studied problem of PoS affiliation and uses misclassification analysis to probe annotation inconsistencies; this framing is useful but not fundamentally new. The claim that PoS information is distributed across many vector components and the emphasis on graded/‘soft’ affiliations offer a noteworthy empirical perspective, but it appears as a moderate extension rather than a novel method given prior work using distributional models for PoS induction and soft categories. Limited scope (only the BNC, no baselines or cross-linguistic validation reported) further suggests the contribution is incremental rather than a breakthrough that would surprise experts.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical question—how much PoS information is encoded in distributional embeddings—and demonstrates useful applications (PoS prediction, highlighting annotation inconsistencies) using the BNC, which makes it potentially influential for corpus linguistics and embedding analysis. However, the lack of baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameter and code release, and experiments limited to a single language reduce confidence that the results will substantially change practices or be widely adopted without further validation. From a reproducibility and generalisability viewpoint these omissions constrain immediate impact, but the ideas do open plausible follow-up directions for cross-lingual and methodological work. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—how distributional models capture part-of-speech categories—and provides empirically useful claims (BNC-trained embeddings reliably encode PoS information, classifiers can detect annotation mismatches, and PoS signal is distributed across many dimensions). These findings are likely to change how linguists and annotators view PoS as graded and distributionally recoverable, and they open clear research directions (cross-linguistic validation, tagset-specific analyses, and tooling for annotation QA). The impact is somewhat constrained by experiments on a single corpus (BNC) and lack of downstream/baseline comparisons, so I see strong but not transformative influence on the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical question—how much PoS information is encoded in distributional embeddings—and demonstrates useful applications (PoS prediction, highlighting annotation inconsistencies) using the BNC, which makes it potentially influential for corpus linguistics and embedding analysis. However, the lack of baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameter and code release, and experiments limited to a single language reduce confidence that the results will substantially change practices or be widely adopted without further validation. From a reproducibility and generalisability viewpoint these omissions constrain immediate impact, but the ideas do open plausible follow-up directions for cross-lingual and methodological work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—how distributional models capture part-of-speech categories—and provides empirically useful claims (BNC-trained embeddings reliably encode PoS information, classifiers can detect annotation mismatches, and PoS signal is distributed across many dimensions). These findings are likely to change how linguists and annotators view PoS as graded and distributionally recoverable, and they open clear research directions (cross-linguistic validation, tagset-specific analyses, and tooling for annotation QA). The impact is somewhat constrained by experiments on a single corpus (BNC) and lack of downstream/baseline comparisons, so I see strong but not transformative influence on the field.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the writing quality is reported as good, with main contributions and limitations stated early (claims about PoS information in BNC-trained embeddings and planned extensions). However, presentation gaps reduce clarity for a reproducibility-minded reader: evaluation metrics and baselines are not specified, hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and it is unclear whether notation/figures are fully defined—details that would help reproduce and interpret the experiments. Overall the narrative is clear but some sections need more concrete methodological and reporting detail. | B(4): From the supplied summary the paper is well organized (intro, methods, experiments, conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early and carried through the analyses, which makes it easy for a linguistically-minded reader to follow. Notation and writing are reported as good, and related work coverage is adequate, but presentation would benefit from more explicit reporting of experimental details (hyperparameters, evaluation metrics) and clearer description of figures/tables or examples that support claims about component importance and misclassification analysis. As a linguistics-oriented reader I found the narrative clear and interpretable, with only minor omissions that hamper exact reproducibility and reduce clarity about some empirical choices.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the writing quality is reported as good, with main contributions and limitations stated early (claims about PoS information in BNC-trained embeddings and planned extensions). However, presentation gaps reduce clarity for a reproducibility-minded reader: evaluation metrics and baselines are not specified, hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and it is unclear whether notation/figures are fully defined—details that would help reproduce and interpret the experiments. Overall the narrative is clear but some sections need more concrete methodological and reporting detail.

reviewer_b.justification: From the supplied summary the paper is well organized (intro, methods, experiments, conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early and carried through the analyses, which makes it easy for a linguistically-minded reader to follow. Notation and writing are reported as good, and related work coverage is adequate, but presentation would benefit from more explicit reporting of experimental details (hyperparameters, evaluation metrics) and clearer description of figures/tables or examples that support claims about component importance and misclassification analysis. As a linguistics-oriented reader I found the narrative clear and interpretable, with only minor omissions that hamper exact reproducibility and reduce clarity about some empirical choices.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper makes clear, plausible claims and provides experiments on BNC showing embeddings encode PoS information and surface interesting outliers, but the experimental support is incomplete: no baselines, no reported evaluation metrics or statistical tests, and hyperparameters/seeds and code are not provided, which harms reproducibility. There are methodological gaps (no controls for frequency/OOV effects, no ablations to show which modeling choices drive results) and the claim about distribution of PoS information across many components is asserted without rigorous component-selection analyses or significance testing. Conclusions are suggestive rather than firmly established given these missing details, so the work is promising but not yet technically conclusive. | B(3): The paper's core empirical claim—that BNC-trained embeddings encode PoS information and can train classifiers to predict PoS and surface annotation inconsistencies—is plausible and supported by experiments described, but the methodology lacks key controls: no baselines, evaluation metrics, statistical tests, or reported hyperparameters, and experiments are limited to English/BNC. There are no mathematical proofs to check, but the absence of standard experimental details and baselines creates logical gaps that weaken confidence in the generality and robustness of the conclusions. Overall, the results are suggestive but require more rigorous evaluation (cross-lingual tests, baselines, and statistical validation) to fully justify the stated claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear, plausible claims and provides experiments on BNC showing embeddings encode PoS information and surface interesting outliers, but the experimental support is incomplete: no baselines, no reported evaluation metrics or statistical tests, and hyperparameters/seeds and code are not provided, which harms reproducibility. There are methodological gaps (no controls for frequency/OOV effects, no ablations to show which modeling choices drive results) and the claim about distribution of PoS information across many components is asserted without rigorous component-selection analyses or significance testing. Conclusions are suggestive rather than firmly established given these missing details, so the work is promising but not yet technically conclusive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's core empirical claim—that BNC-trained embeddings encode PoS information and can train classifiers to predict PoS and surface annotation inconsistencies—is plausible and supported by experiments described, but the methodology lacks key controls: no baselines, evaluation metrics, statistical tests, or reported hyperparameters, and experiments are limited to English/BNC. There are no mathematical proofs to check, but the absence of standard experimental details and baselines creates logical gaps that weaken confidence in the generality and robustness of the conclusions. Overall, the results are suggestive but require more rigorous evaluation (cross-lingual tests, baselines, and statistical validation) to fully justify the stated claims.

main_claims: Word embeddings trained on the British National Corpus contain robust information about part-of-speech (PoS) affiliation.

Distributional semantic models can be used to train classifiers to predict PoS tags for words and to detect words whose distributional patterns differ from their annotated PoS, revealing annotation inconsistencies.
PoS information in embeddings is distributed across many vector components (dozens to hundreds), not confined to one or two dimensions.
Distributional models reflect graded or ‘soft’ part-of-speech affiliations and can support linguistic hypotheses about PoS class changes.
_summary.methodology: Train distributional vector models (word embeddings) on the British National Corpus and train classifiers that predict PoS tags for words based on their embeddings; analyze resulting clusters and misclassified/outlier words to identify annotation inconsistencies and study component importance.
limitations: Experiments were conducted only on English (BNC) and authors plan to extend to typologically different languages.
Distributional models are trained in a nondeterministic and stochastic way, which may affect results.
Part-of-speech boundaries are not strict/categorical, which complicates clear-cut classification.

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics who has led reproducible NLP benchmarking projects and implemented large-scale distributional models in production.
traits: checks for clear baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance when empirical claims are made
prioritizes reproducibility: wants training data/preprocessing details, hyperparameters, random seeds, and code release
evaluates experimental design sensitivity: probes robustness to corpus size, embedding architecture, and tagset choices
insists on appropriate evaluation metrics and controls for confounds (e.g., frequency effects, OOVs, homographs)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments truly support the claims and whether results can be trusted and reproduced in practice.

reviewer_b.name: Linguistic Impact Analyst
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP and cognitive linguistics who studies part-of-speech theory, linguistic annotation practices, and the application of distributional semantics to linguistic description.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior computational and linguistic work on PoS induction and soft categories
values clear interpretation of results for linguists: which hypotheses are supported and what the analyses contribute to theory or annotation practice
looks for broader applicability and limitations: how findings transfer across languages, tagsets, corpora, and downstream tasks
notices the connection between quantitative patterns and existing linguistic typologies or known annotation artifacts
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's conceptual contribution and potential impact on linguistic theory and annotation practice, weighing interpretability and generality of results.
